content:
written on July 21, 2026

Two recent
stories
about Silicon Valley are hard to read without wondering if everyone involved has
lost their mind. Once a place that celebrated “the crazy ones, the misfits and
rebels who saw things differently,” it now seems to have found a new kind of
crazy that isn’t born of rebellion but fear of falling behind. Everyone is
chasing the person ahead of them, with little time to remember where they meant
to go.

One story talks about a couple that earns a combined $550,000 a year and still
feels behind because someone at Anthropic might buy the dream house first. The
husband asked his wife to take over almost all parenting so he could spend days,
nights and weekends becoming his company’s top user of AI. And she did.

The other story covers a founder who has Claude review her first dates and tell
her whether she was engaging and empathetic enough, thanks to recording every
single one of them. Two people meet and afterwards, rather than talk to the
other person or trust her own judgment, one of them sits alone with a machine
and asks how well she performed.

Technology was supposed to make room for life but instead for more and more
people life is slowly being rearranged around AI. People fear being replaced by
machines and respond by giving those machines more of their judgment, attention
and time. And for what? Every “saved” hour is returned to a race with no
finish line.

If the valley shows us what it means to live in the future, perhaps falling
behind is not the worst thing that could happen. Maybe we need the misfits who
know when to stop. Who leave a conversation unrecorded. Who play with their
children without agents running in the background. Who can admire another
person’s success without feeling diminished by it. Who find peace not by
getting ahead, but by no longer needing to.

The Great Buddha of Kamakura. (Wikimedia Commons.)

This entry was tagged
ai and
culture
